transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Thank you for calling Sunrise Recovery Center, this is Isaac. How can I help?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Hi, I'm Monica. I'm calling about my friend. Her name is Simone. She asked me to call for her because she was too nervous to do it herself.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: I'm glad she has someone like you to help her take this step. Before I go further — just so you both understand — I can share general information with you, but because Simone isn't a family member, I'd want to involve her directly before we discuss her specific situation in any detail. Is she with you right now or available to speak?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: She's right here. She can hear you — I have you on speaker.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Simone, can you hear me okay? I just want to make sure you're comfortable with your friend being part of this call.

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Yes. I'm here. I asked Monica to help me. I'm just really scared.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: That's completely okay, Simone. You don't have to do any of the talking if you don't want to. I'm glad you're on the line. Can you tell me, in your own words, what's been going on?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: I've been using cocaine for about eighteen months. It's gotten out of control. I spend money I don't have. I've been lying to my family.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Thank you for telling me that. Eighteen months and the pattern you're describing — the financial consequences, the hiding — those are signs that this has moved beyond something you can manage on your own, and reaching out today is exactly the right move. Simone, are you also drinking or using anything else alongside the cocaine?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Sometimes I drink. To come down.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Okay. And have you tried to stop on your own?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: A few times. I can't get past a week.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: That's a really common pattern with stimulant use and it doesn't mean you lack willpower — it means your brain has adapted and needs support to reset. Do you have health insurance, Simone?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Yes. Summit Select. I have my card. Member ID BP34584961, group GRP-321429.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Thank you. Here's the plan: I'll verify your benefits and have our admissions team call you — Simone, on your number directly, unless you'd prefer Monica to be included in that call too.

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: I'd like her on the call.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Then I'll note that. You'll hear from us within ninety minutes. Simone — having a friend willing to make this call with you is not a small thing. Hold onto that.

gold_labels.coaching_notes: Strong handling of the friend-caller edge case — agent immediately established consent by addressing the patient directly, confirmed she was comfortable with the arrangement, and then pivoted naturally to the patient for the clinical questions. Next step correctly offered patient the choice of including friend in follow-up call. Family_caller_handling scored 5 because the non-family consent situation was navigated without making it feel legalistic.
